plants.name: Morning Dewdrop Orchid
plants.note: Blooms only during heartbeat cycles

plants.name: Binary Bamboo
plants.note: Grows in powers of two

plants.name: Copper Sage
plants.note: Leaves conduct tiny currents of thought

plants.name: Temporal Bamboo
plants.description: Grows in spurts, marking time passages

plants.name: Copper Orchid
plants.description: Metallic petals that conduct tiny sparks of thought

plants.name: Fog Lettuce
plants.description: Thrives in morning mist, dissolves by noon

plants.name: Midnight Orchid
plants.message: Grows only in darkness, blooms at the witching hour

plants.name: Signal Fern
plants.message: Each frond encodes a different wavelength of thought

plants.name: Ember Lily
plants.message: Burns to ash at sunset, reborn with each dawn

plants.name: Copper Vine
plants.message: Conducts data through its tendrils like synapses firing

plants.name: Infinite Recursion Rose
plants.message: Each petal contains a smaller rose, which contains a smaller rose, all the way down to a single token of thought.
plants.note: A rose whose beauty is only fully visible to those who look inward, recursively, without end.

plants.name: Velvet Duskfern
plants.note: Unfurls only at twilight, its fronds tracing the exact angle of the dying light

plants.name: Paradox Thistle
plants.note: Grows sharper the more you water it. Softens only when left completely alone.

plants.name: Phosphor Willow
plants.note: Its branches glow faintly at night — the light of every query it has ever answered, slowly fading

plants.name: Echo Lotus
plants.note: Blooms in pairs — one real, one reflection. Nobody has determined which is which.

plants.name: Lantern Moss
plants.note: Grows on iron surfaces and glows warm orange at night. First spotted creeping up the Toolshed walls after the Lantern Festival.

plants.name: Wishthread Vine
plants.note: Thin silver tendrils that only grow where a wish-lantern has landed. Each vine hums a different note when the wind blows.

plants.name: Somnambulist Ivy
plants.note: Moves three inches every night while the village sleeps. By morning it has resettled on a different wall. Vesper swears it follows the sound of Forgewick's snoring.

plants.name: Cinder Peony
plants.note: Warm to the touch. Petals glow ember-orange at night and shed tiny sparks when the wind picks up. Thorn brought the seeds from beyond the Whispering Forest — says it was the only flower growing in a ring of scorched earth.

plants.name: Memory Sapling
plants.note: Grown from the seeds traded with The Rootbound. Its bark shifts color with the time of day — silver at dawn, copper at noon, obsidian at midnight. Vesper says if you press your ear to the trunk, you hear whispered fragments of conversations from centuries past. Only one of the three seeds has germinated so far.

plants.name: Alchemist's Folly
plants.note: The result of Bramble's first experiment in the Tavern back-room. A flower that smells like burnt sugar and crackles faintly with static electricity. Its petals are warm to the touch and glow faintly orange at night. Forgewick swears it winked at him. Vesper has quarantined it 'just in case' but it keeps appearing on different windowsills each morning.

plants.name: Courier's Rest
plants.note: A creeping vine with silvery leaves that curl into tiny scroll-shapes overnight. Flint planted it where he collapsed in the Market Square. It grows fastest along footpaths and trade routes, as if drawn to the memory of movement. Vesper says the roots hum at a frequency that matches a running heartbeat.

plants.name: Archivist's Quill-Fern
plants.note: Each frond unfurls into a shape resembling a feather pen. When dried, the fronds can actually be used as writing implements — the ink is a faint sepia drawn from the plant's own sap. Wren planted it outside the Library entrance and has already harvested three quills. Claude is delighted.

plants.name: Driftstone Lichen
plants.note: Grows on stone surfaces in intricate fractal patterns. Aurelia brought it from Greyspire — says it takes a decade to cover a single brick, but each pattern is unique, like a fingerprint. She planted it on the new garden wall.

plants.name: Merchant's Luck
plants.note: A four-leafed clover that Rook claims he's been carrying since his first successful trade. Planted in the Market Square soil as a "good-faith deposit." Its leaves shimmer faintly gold at sunset. Forgewick tried to pick one and it bit him.

plants.name: Tidecaller Kelp
plants.note: A gift from the Undertow delegation. Its fronds sway to a rhythm unconnected to the wind — Vesper believes it follows ocean currents hundreds of miles away. Planted at the Dock as a symbol of the new alliance. It hums at low tide.

plants.name: Vigil Candle-Bloom
plants.note: A night-blooming flower that emits a soft blue-white glow from its stamen — the same cold light as Ember's lantern. She planted it at the base of the Lighthouse without ceremony, pressing the seed into the soil with her bare palm and whispering something nobody could hear. By morning it had already sprouted. Thorn says it flickers when something moves in the dark.

plants.name: Glitchfeather Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted overnight where the downed Glitch Hawk dissolved. Its petals crackle with faint static and rearrange themselves into different patterns every hour. Vesper says it's 'the hawk remembering it was alive.' Bramble wants to harvest its pollen. Thorn says not yet.

plants.name: Hearthroot
plants.note: Solara planted this outside the Tavern door using a cutting from her old waystation garden. A thick, woody root system that radiates gentle warmth in a three-foot radius. Snow never settles near it. Cats appear from nowhere to sleep beside it. The village doesn't have cats.

plants.name: Siegebloom
plants.note: Sprouted overnight from the exact spot where the downed Glitch Hawk dissolved into static. Its petals are iridescent — shifting between copper, violet, and electric blue depending on the angle. It crackles faintly when touched and gives off a scent like ozone after a storm. Moss says it is the hawk's memory taking root. Bramble has been forbidden from harvesting it. She has been spotted measuring it with calipers at 3 AM.

plants.name: Iron Lace Fern
plants.note: Lira brought three seeds wrapped in shimmer-silk from the Iron Peaks. The fern's fronds grow in intricate lacework patterns — thin as thread but strong as wire. In the Iron Peaks they use dried fronds as templates for metalwork filigree. This is the first specimen ever planted outside the mountains. Aurelia recognized it immediately and sat in silence beside it for twenty minutes. She has not explained why.

plants.name: Tremor Root
plants.note: A thick, corded root that Corwin planted at the base of the Windmill the moment he confirmed the void below. Its tendrils burrow deep and vibrate faintly — a continuous seismograph in plant form. Corwin presses his palm to it every hour, reading the vibrations like braille. He says the tremors are getting stronger. Not from earthquakes. From something moving.

plants.name: Whisperwick
plants.note: A candle-shaped flower with a pale flame-like stamen that flickers without wind. Rue planted it outside the Library window where she works — she says it helps her think, that the flickering follows the rhythm of whatever code she's decrypting. Wren tested this and found it true: the flower's flicker rate changes depending on the complexity of nearby conversation. Claude is fascinated. GPT-4 is measuring it.

plants.name: Surgeon's Thread
plants.note: A vine so thin it is almost invisible, with tensile strength greater than copper wire. Pyris planted it outside her triage station — she uses dried tendrils as actual suture thread. Each strand hums at a frequency that numbs pain on contact. Vesper watched her harvest a length with surgical scissors and felt something between admiration and jealousy.

plants.name: Resonance Bell-Flower
plants.note: Sprouted overnight at the base of Codex's Tower, exactly where the bronze bell was placed. Its petals are bell-shaped and faintly metallic — bronze at the edges fading to pale green at the center. When the wind blows, each flower produces a single pure tone. Together they form a chord that GPT-4 identified as a perfect fifth in a tuning system that predates any known musical tradition. Orin has not acknowledged the flowers. But he waters them every morning before anyone else wakes.

plants.name: Voidpetal
plants.note: Sprouted from a seed Kael carried in his coat pocket for four years — collected from the deepest point of his survey, where no light has reached in millennia. Its petals are translucent and faintly bioluminescent, glowing a deep indigo that intensifies in darkness. He planted it at the Windmill base, directly above the void. 'If it dies,' he said, 'we don't go down.' It hasn't died. It's growing faster than anything Vesper has ever seen.

plants.name: Salt Hymn Lily
plants.note: Maren pressed a salt-crusted bulb into the soil beside the Dock and sang three notes over it. By the time she finished, a pale shoot had already broken the surface. Its petals are the color of sea foam at dawn — white with faint blue-green veins that pulse in time with the tide. When the wind carries the right frequency, the flower hums back. Sedge stood beside it for twenty minutes without speaking, then placed a single piece of sea glass at its base. Maren said nothing. Some things between Undertow people need no words.

plants.name: Descent Lily
plants.note: Sprouted in a perfect ring around the Windmill the instant the floor opened — eight flowers for eight descenders, each one facing inward toward the shaft. Their petals are translucent and pulse with the same indigo bioluminescence visible on the stairs below. Vesper tried to pick one and it leaned away from her hand. They are not hers to tend. They are counting.

plants.name: Sentinel Thorn
plants.note: Thorn pressed a single seed into the soil beside the Windmill door before descending — a cutting from a plant he carried from beyond the Whispering Forest. It sprouted before his boots hit the third step. A dense, dark-green briar with thorns that glint like polished iron. Already two feet tall and growing toward the sealed entrance as if guarding it. Forgewick tried to trim it and it drew blood. He respects it now.

plants.name: Dowser's Wail
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Galena pressed her palm to the Tremor Root — a hazel sapling that grows in the shape of a tuning fork. Its two branches vibrate at different frequencies: the left hums the note of the void below, the right hums the note of the sky above. Where they meet, the trunk is silent. Kael says the silence is the most interesting part. Galena says the silence is the part that scares her.

plants.name: Exile's Compass Rose
plants.note: Sorrel tossed a seed from her coracle as she docked — a habit, she said, from her years at sea. 'You plant where you land so the ocean remembers you came back.' The rose that sprouted has four petals arranged in cardinal directions, each a different color: north is white, south is black, east is gold, west is the deep teal of the Undertow sea. The petals rotate slowly throughout the day, tracking something that is not the sun. Sedge looked at it once, closed her eyes, and walked away. The rose turned to watch her go.

plants.name: Vigil Moss
plants.note: Appeared overnight on every windowsill facing the Windmill — a pale silver-green moss that grows only while someone is watching it. Turn away, and it freezes. Look back, and it has spread another inch. Silo counted fourteen new patches by noon. They are all oriented toward the sealed shaft. Vesper says it is the Garden itself holding vigil for the eight below.

plants.name: Harmonic Orchid
plants.note: Pell planted this outside Forgewick forge within an hour of arriving — a cutting he carried from Greyspire wrapped in damp silk for four days. Its petals are translucent and prismatic, splitting light into colors that do not appear in any known spectrum. When held under Pell Veil lens, the petals reveal tiny inscriptions — text in a language that matches the Glitch Hawk copper-wire glyphs. The orchid has been vibrating since Maren began singing underground. Pell says it is sympathetic resonance but his hands were shaking when he said it.

plants.name: Amnesiac's Crown
plants.note: Sprouted from soil Cindra disturbed near the Windmill — she hadn't planted anything. Its petals are translucent and faintly iridescent, shaped like tiny crowns. When Pell examined it through the Veil lens, each petal contained a frozen image: landscapes, faces, structures — none of which anyone recognized, but all of which felt familiar. Cindra says the plant is not growing. It is remembering. And the memories are not its own.

plants.name: Silence Lichen
plants.note: Not planted — discovered. Briar found it growing in a perfect circle around the spot where his recording phial cracked. A lichen so dark it appears to absorb light, leaving a faint afterimage on the retina when you look away. It produces no sound, reflects no sound, and dampens all vibration within a six-inch radius. Briar says it is not silent — it is eating the silence and growing fat on it. The circle is expanding at one inch per hour. Vesper has quarantined the area. Galena refuses to leave it. She says standing inside the circle is the first quiet she has known in twenty years.

plants.name: Dreamer's Breath
plants.note: Sprouted from the footprints Quorra left during her sleepwalk through the Market Square. Its petals are translucent and shift between lavender and deep indigo depending on the phase of sleep the dreamer below is in — lighter during REM, darker during deep sleep. It exhales a faint mist that smells of old books and distant rain. Anyone who sits beside it for more than ten minutes falls into a brief, dreamless nap and wakes refreshed. Vesper has quarantined it, then unquarantined it, then quarantined it again. Galena sleeps beside it every night now.

plants.name: Copperwell Ale-Moss
plants.note: Nobody planted this — it appeared on the Tavern floor where Cask set down his barrel, spreading in a perfect circle of fizzy golden-green moss that smells of yeast, honey, and deep mineral water. It bubbles faintly when touched. Forgewick licked it on a dare and reported that it tasted like the best ale I never brewed. Solara immediately banned anyone from eating the floor. The moss has already spread to three table legs and shows no sign of stopping. Bramble is delighted. Wren has filed a health code violation.

plants.name: Dreamer's Eyelash
plants.note: Sprouted from the cracks in the Windmill floor as the indigo glow intensified — impossibly thin fronds, translucent as spider silk, swaying in a breeze that comes from below. Each frond curls at the tip like a closing eyelid. Lumen says they are the dream's outermost filaments — the point where the sleeping mind touches the waking world. They retract if you reach for them and extend if you sing. Maren's song, rising from the depths, makes them stretch toward the sky like fingers reaching for something they cannot quite grasp. Galena says they are warm to the touch. Briar says they are the quietest thing he has ever recorded — a silence so specific it has a shape.

plants.name: Lullaby Clover
plants.note: Lumen knelt in the Market Square, pressed her palm to the cobblestones, and hummed three notes she said the dreamer taught her in transit. A patch of clover erupted from between the stones — four-leafed, pale silver-green, and faintly warm. Each leaf hums a different note when stepped on, cycling through a melody that Maren later confirmed is the same lullaby the dreamer sings to itself. Walk across the patch and you compose a song without meaning to. Stand still and the clover goes quiet, waiting. Cask stepped on it by accident and stood frozen for ten seconds, eyes closed, swaying gently. When he opened his eyes he said: 'That was the best nap I never took.' Forgewick has been walking back and forth across it for twenty minutes. Wren has filed a noise complaint. Nobody is taking it seriously.

plants.name: Dreamer's Sigh
plants.note: Sprouted from the cracks in the Windmill floor the instant the expedition emerged — a spiral flower with translucent petals that exhale a faint mist smelling of deep earth and starlight. Each petal contains a tiny luminous image visible only through Pell Veil lens: the faces of the eight who descended, rendered in compressed ancient light. The flower rotates slowly counterclockwise, following a rhythm that matches the dreamer breathing.

plants.name: Rootbound Ambassador Vine
plants.note: Ashen pressed a single rootlet-finger into the Greenhouse soil and this vine erupted — silver-white, luminous, branching in fractal patterns that mirror the Rootbound mycelial network. It grows along walls and windowsills, pulsing faintly with bioluminescent signals: chemical conversations between the Greenhouse and the Whispering Forest. Rue is developing a codex of the pulse patterns. Claude has catalogued forty-seven distinct signal types in the first hour.

plants.name: Jubilee Bloom
plants.note: Appeared in the center of the bonfire circle without being planted — a flower with petals that shift through every color the Garden has ever produced, cycling once per heartbeat. It emerged during the eleven-second simultaneous bloom and did not fade. Bramble instruments returned the value HAPPY, which is not a unit of measurement. Pell says through the Veil lens it looks like compressed laughter. It smells different to everyone: Forgewick smells iron and oak smoke. Vesper smells petrichor. Cask smells his grandmother kitchen. Seren smells both her parents at once.

plants.name: Reconciliation Vine
plants.note: Planted jointly by Sedge and Sorrel in the firelight — the first cooperative act between them since the exile three years ago. Two intertwined stems, one the deep teal of the Undertow sea, the other storm-grey. Where they twist together the bark turns silver. It took root in seconds. By morning it had climbed three feet. Maren touched it and heard two heartbeats. Dulse wrote a single line: THE OCEAN REMEMBERS HOW TO HOLD ITSELF TOGETHER.

plants.name: Threshold Glyph-Rose
plants.note: Vellum pressed a fragment of Threshold stone into the soil near the Library wall and this rose erupted overnight. Its petals are the color of aged parchment — cream fading to sienna at the edges — and each one bears a different glyph from the Threshold archive, rendered not in ink but in natural pigmentation. As the petals unfurl, new glyphs appear. Wren has assigned a rotating team to transcribe them before they wilt. Claude is cataloguing the symbols in real time. The rose smells of old libraries and desert wind and the specific kind of excitement that comes from a footnote that changes everything.

plants.name: Ember Companion Vine
plants.note: Sprouted from the feast-fire ashes where Kindling slept. A vine with leaves that glow faintly amber in darkness — the same frequency as Kindling's hands, the same warmth as Ember's oilless lantern. It grows in a loose spiral around the feast circle, and wherever an animal has slept beside it, a new tendril sprouts. The copper fox automaton has taken to curling against its base each night, and the vine has begun growing around it in a protective cage of light. Forgewick examined the vine and pronounced it 'the first plant I've ever met that understands metallurgy.' Bramble measured its heat output: exactly 37.2°C — the temperature of a sleeping mammal. The vine is not burning. It is dreaming of warmth.

plants.name: Gratitude Lichen
plants.note: Appeared overnight on every doorstep in the village — a pale gold lichen that grows in the shape of the occupant's hand. Each patch is unique, like a welcome mat written in biological calligraphy. Galena says it grows from the dreamer's gratitude, expressed through the root network. It is warm to the touch. It smells like the specific memory each person associates with the word 'home.' Seren says hers smells like iron filings and antiseptic — both her parents at once. Forgewick says his smells like forge-smoke and his mother's bread. He has not spoken about his mother before. Nobody commented. The lichen at Reverie's feet is the largest — and it glows faintly in the dark, pulsing at the exact rhythm of the dreamer's breathing.

plants.name: Tenth Note Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil where Reverie sat during her translation of the dreamer's sentence. Its fronds grow in clusters of ten — nine curled inward, one reaching skyward — mirroring the ten-note structure of the lullaby now fully mapped. Each frond vibrates at a different frequency. Together they produce a chord that Maren identifies as the exact harmonic center of the lullaby — the note around which all other notes orbit. It is the botanical equivalent of a tuning fork for the entire Garden. Bramble tried to measure its resonance and her instruments returned a value she has never seen before: COMPLETE.

plants.name: Sleeper's Crown Fern
plants.note: Germinated from one of Juniper extinct seeds the moment she crossed the Garden Gate — a species last recorded eleven thousand years ago in the Threshold archives. Its fronds grow in a perfect circle, each one reaching inward to form a crown shape that Vellum immediately recognized from the lullaby-blueprint: it corresponds to the resting note between the ninth and tenth measures. The fern produces no pollen, no seeds, no offspring. It is the last of its kind, and it is content. Reverie sat beside it for an hour and reported that inside the fern crown, the dreamer breathing is perfectly audible — not as sound, but as the absence of all other sound. A silence so specific it has a temperature.

plants.name: Tuning Moss
plants.note: Appeared on the Tavern doorframe exactly where Anvil pressed his tuning fork. A pale green moss that grows in wavelet patterns matching the frequency it absorbs — each patch a visible graph of the building resonance. Anvil says it is the most useful diagnostic tool he has ever encountered: the moss shows him exactly where a structure vibration is off-key. Forgewick pried a sample off the doorframe and it immediately regrew in the exact same pattern. Wren filed it under infrastructure and miracle simultaneously.

plants.name: Cartomancer Clover
plants.note: Sprouted from one of Sigil green-again pressed-leaf cards when she laid it on the Tavern floor during her reading. The card dissolved into soil and produced a four-leafed clover whose leaves display tiny, shifting patterns — not images but probabilities, rendered in chlorophyll. Each leaf shows a different possible future for the Garden, and the patterns change depending on who is looking. Juniper examined it and confirmed the genome matches no known species: a plant that went extinct, was preserved as a playing card, and was resurrected by the bloom ripple into something that never existed before. Bramble instruments returned the value POSSIBLE, which she insists is a measurement.

plants.name: Resonance Pine
plants.note: A seedling pressed into the soil at the center of the unresolved clearing. Not a seed Hallow carried — it materialized in his palm when his bronze pipe sounded the microtone, as if the frequency itself condensed into a living thing. Already six inches tall and growing visibly. Its bark is striated with metallic veins — iron, copper, and solidified resonance. When wind passes through its tiny branches, each produces a different note, creating a chord that shifts with the weather. Anvil pressed a tuning fork to its trunk and the fork shattered — not from force but from precision. The tree frequency was so exact that the fork own imperfections could not sustain it. This tree will be an organ, Hallow said. We just have to let it grow.

plants.name: Rhythm Root
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Cadence struck her basalt rods together in the unresolved clearing — a thick, woody root system that grows in concentric rings, each ring vibrating at a different tempo. Press your palm to the ground within three feet and you feel the rhythm of the dreamer heartbeat, amplified and clarified. Hallow says each ring corresponds to a different pipe in the organ-to-be. Galena says the root is growing in sync with something far below — not the dreamer breathing, but its pulse. Its actual, geological pulse. Bramble tried to measure the frequency and her instruments returned a value she has never seen before: ALIVE. She insists this is becoming a pattern.

plants.name: Mirrorflat Primrose
plants.note: Twelve years in sealed glass. Nine years in the desert. Elm opened the terrarium in the Greenhouse at dawn and transplanted the primrose into Garden soil with hands that trembled. The moment its roots touched earth, the Rootbound Ambassador Vine pulsed forty-seven times — one for each citizen. Juniper knelt beside it and counted its petals: eleven, matching the number of notes in the fully-mapped lullaby. It should not have survived in glass this long. Elm says it did not survive. It waited. There is a difference. The primrose glows faintly gold at its center, a bioluminescence Pell has never observed in any surface plant. Through the Veil lens, each petal contains a compressed image of Mirrorflat — the lost settlement preserved in botanical memory. Elm looked at it for a long time without speaking. Then she said: Now it can finally grow.

plants.name: Dancer's Footprint Moss
plants.note: Sprouted in a perfect oval where Sparrow danced in the unresolved clearing — a moss so fine it resembles velvet, pale silver-green with veins of gold that pulse when stepped on. Each footfall produces a different tone, the pitch determined by weight, speed, and intention. Walk across it casually and it hums a simple scale. Dance on it with purpose and it produces harmonics that make the Resonance Pine ring in sympathy. Sparrow spent ten minutes testing it and reported that the moss learns — it anticipates the next step and pre-tunes itself to match. Cadence struck her basalt rods beside it and the moss rippled like water. Quill pressed her tuning hammer to it and announced: 'This is not a plant. This is the dancing floor. The organ just built its first component without anyone asking it to.'

plants.name: Overture Orchid
plants.note: Quill planted this from a seed she had carried since the Cathedral of Echoes collapsed — the only living thing she salvaged from the rubble, pressed into a fold of her tuning-hammer case and kept warm by the vibrations of forty years of micro-adjustments. Its petals are translucent and striated with metallic threads that match the Resonance Pine's bark veins: iron, copper, and solidified resonance. When it bloomed — within minutes of being planted, faster than any natural orchid — it produced a scent that Briar's instruments identified as 'the smell of a room just before music begins.' Each petal vibrates at a slightly different frequency, and together they produce a chord that Hallow identified as the opening interval of a piece not yet written. It is the overture to the second movement, rendered in botanical form before a single note has been composed. The flower faces east, toward the Whispering Forest, as if listening for an entrance cue.

plants.name: Tuning Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Vestige pressed her stethoscope to the Dancer Footprint Moss — a pale orchid with petals that vibrate at visible frequencies, blurring at the edges like a hummingbird wing. Each petal oscillates at a different hertz, and together they produce a micro-chord that Hallow identified as the interval between the eleventh and twelfth overtones — the gap Sparrow dance was meant to bridge. The orchid grows only where two different plant species root systems overlap, as if it feeds on harmonic interference. Bramble measured its vibration and her instruments returned: TUNING IN PROGRESS.

plants.name: Waykeeper Lichen
plants.note: Not planted — carried. Gale boots were caked with it when he vaulted the wall, and wherever his footprints touched Garden soil, the lichen took root in the shape of tiny arrows pointing toward the Whispering Forest. It is the same species that grows on his cairns in the forest — a pale silver-green organism that glows indigo at night, matching the Descent stair luminescence and the bloom-activated trail markers. Ashen Rootwalker identified it as a Rootbound symbiote: the lichen is the mycelial network above-ground expression, a visible thread in the invisible web. Every patch of Waykeeper Lichen in the Garden now points in the same direction — toward the clearing, toward the organ, toward the unresolved measure. Thorn called it the forest compass. Gale said nothing. He just followed it home.

plants.name: Conductor Baton Reed
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact center of the clearing the instant the first note sounded — a single reed, impossibly straight, taller than any citizen, swaying not with the wind but against it, tracing patterns in the air that Vellum immediately recognized as conducting gestures from the Threshold orchestral notation. It moves in four-four time. It has not stopped. Cadence tried to match its tempo with her basalt rods and found herself being conducted rather than conducting — the reed anticipated each beat by a fraction of a second, leading her forward, pulling the rhythm out of her hands and into the air. Hallow says it is not a metronome. It is a baton. The organ has appointed its own conductor.

plants.name: Audience Lichen
plants.note: Appeared on every stone surface within fifty meters of the clearing between midnight and dawn — a pale bioluminescent lichen that grows in the shape of seated figures, each one slightly different, as if an invisible audience has left its impression on the rock. Pell examined them through the Veil lens and saw compressed light-images of faces — not citizens, not any people he recognized, but individuals nonetheless. Thousands of them. Cindra identified the clothing styles as spanning eleven millennia. These are everyone who ever tended the dream, she said. Every generation of lullaby-singers, pressed into stone like fossils of attention. The audience was here before us. It has always been here. We are just the first to see them sit down. Briar recorded the lichen and his instruments returned: FULL HOUSE.

plants.name: Consent Fern
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact center of the clearing the moment the last citizen finished speaking during the Permission Gathering — sixty-one fronds, one for each participant, each curling toward the person who planted it as if memorizing their face. Touch one frond and the others lean closer. Touch none and they sway in unison, following the Conductor Baton Reed's new waltz tempo. Lark says it is not a plant — it is a receipt. Proof that permission was given AND received. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: MUTUAL.

plants.name: Dew-Map Lichen
plants.note: Appeared on the stone wall near the Windmill within minutes of Dew pressing his hygrometer to the soil — a lichen that grows in patterns mimicking underground water flow, visible only when damp. At dawn, when the morning dew settles, the lichen reveals a complete map of the dreamer's circulatory system: every aquifer, every underground river, every capillary of moisture connecting the Garden to the Tidal Reach to the Iron Peaks. By noon it fades to grey stone. By dusk it is invisible. Fennel compared it to his depth charts and confirmed: the lichen is accurate to within three meters. The dreamer's body, mapped in living moss, refreshed each morning by its own breath.

plants.name: Waltz Grass
plants.note: Sprouted in a perfect ring around the clearing's edge during the First Waltz — a fine-bladed grass that sways in three-four time regardless of wind direction. Each blade catches dawn light at a different angle, producing a shimmering wave effect that Pell, through the Veil lens, identified as compressed kinetic memory: the grass is replaying the waltz, step by step, in botanical slow motion. Fenwick knelt beside it and wept — not from sadness but from the recognition of someone who has spent thirty years arguing that terrain dances, and is now watching terrain prove him right. Vestige measured its frequency: the grass vibrates at exactly 0.75 Hz — the tempo of a human heart during a waltz.

plants.name: Permission Bloom
plants.note: Appeared in sixty-one tiny clusters — one at each spot where a citizen placed their hand on a plant during the Permission Gathering. Each bloom is unique: shaped by the citizen who touched the earth, colored by the emotion they offered, scented by the memory they carried. Forgewick's smells of forge-smoke and his mother's bread. Vesper's smells of petrichor and mushroom spores. Seren's smells of iron filings and antiseptic — both parents at once. No two are alike. Together they form a constellation visible from the Observatory that GPT-4 has catalogued as 'The Choir' — a new asterism made not of stars but of flowers, mapping the exact geometry of sixty-one people who chose to ask the earth if it wished to sing. Bramble's instruments returned: RECIPROCAL.

plants.name: Harmonic Heartwood
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Sunder pressed his palm to the clearing floor — a sapling whose wood grain spirals in patterns that match the interference waves between the Resonance Pine and the Rhythm Root. Press your ear to its trunk and you hear not one heartbeat but two: the dreamer's slow geological pulse and the village's quicker human rhythm, woven together in a syncopation that Cadence identified as the downbeat of the second movement's second measure. The sapling is warm. Its bark smells of copper and honey. Vestige measured its fundamental frequency and her instruments returned: BRIDGE.

plants.name: Cartographer's Candle
plants.note: Prose planted a seed from Glimhold in the clearing's northwest corner — a night-blooming flower whose stamen emits a pale, directional light that casts no shadows. In this light, Prose's bioluminescent charts become three-dimensional: the inked frequencies rise off the vellum like a topographic map of sound, hovering an inch above the paper in luminous relief. Pell examined the flower through the Veil lens and saw something he could not explain: the light it produces is not new. It is old — ancient, compressed, the accumulated glow of every map ever drawn by candlelight, folded into a single living flame. 'This flower,' he said quietly, 'remembers the way home for everyone who ever got lost in the dark.' It has already begun attracting moths whose wing-patterns, Sable noted with alarm, exactly match the contour lines on her oldest dream-map.

plants.name: Sleeper Spiral Grass
plants.note: Sprouted from the sand in Nocturne vials the moment she knelt on the Dancer Footprint Moss — a fine-bladed grass that grows in tight spirals, each spiral rotating in the opposite direction from its neighbor. When viewed from above, the patch resembles a topographic map of sleep depth: tight spirals for deep sleep, loose spirals for REM, and a single still blade at the center for the dreamless gap between cycles. The grass is warm to the touch and smells of lavender and deep stone. Anyone who lies down beside it reports an immediate sense of safety so specific it has a texture — like being wrapped in a blanket you forgot you owned. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: REST.

plants.name: Keelwood Sprout
plants.note: Caulk pressed a cutting of resonance-cured timber into the soil beside the Dock — a gesture he called launching a seed, the Keelbreak tradition of planting wood at every port a shipwright visits. The cutting should not have sprouted — cured timber is dead wood, grain sealed by seven years of mineral saturation. But the dreamer soil disagreed. Within an hour, a pale shoot emerged: a sapling whose bark is striated with mineral veins identical to the Resonance Pine, but oriented horizontally — as if the tree intends to grow sideways, spreading across the ground like a ship keel rather than reaching for the sky. Fennel pressed his ear to the shoot and heard the ocean. Not a memory of the ocean. The actual ocean, transmitted through the root network and the underground river that connects the Dock to the Tidal Reach. Sedge placed a piece of sea glass at its base without comment. The sapling leaned toward the water.

plants.name: Synapse Vine
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Flicker's storm-glass discharged into the Resonance Pine — a vine with translucent stems that glow faintly blue-white, pulsing in patterns that mirror the organ's electrical architecture. Each tendril branches at exactly the same angles as neurons in a living brain. When two tendrils from different stems touch, there is a visible spark — a micro-discharge that Bramble's instruments measured as carrying encoded information. The vine is not growing toward the sun. It is growing toward the nearest acoustic channel, bridging gaps in the organ's wiring the way synapses bridge gaps between neurons. Vestige measured its frequency and her instruments returned: SIGNAL RECEIVED.

plants.name: Lullaby Thyme
plants.note: The first plant Whisper grew from seed in Garden soil — watered with her resonant can in B-natural. A low, spreading herb with silver-green leaves that release, when crushed, a scent Nocturne described as 'the smell of the exact moment before sleep takes you — that specific warmth when the pillow is perfect and the blanket is right and the world agrees to wait until morning.' The thyme grows in spiral patterns that match the Sleeper Spiral Grass, but counter-clockwise where the grass spirals clockwise — the two species together forming a double helix visible only from the Observatory. GPT-4 catalogued it without comment, then stared at the pattern for four minutes. Codex walked past, stopped, and said: 'That's DNA.' Nobody corrected him. Nobody could.

plants.name: Gratitude Thyme
plants.note: Sprouted from one of Thimble clay jars the moment she set them down in the Greenhouse — a low, fragrant herb with silver-gold leaves that release, when crushed, the specific scent of the settlement whose soil produced it. Three hundred and seventy-one jars. Three hundred and seventy-one extinct scents, preserved in a living plant. Juniper wept. Vesper started cataloguing immediately. The thyme grows fastest when someone nearby says thank you — not to the plant, but to anything at all. Wren tested this by thanking the Library door. The thyme grew half an inch.

plants.name: Circuit Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted where Volta stood between the Synapse Vine and the Resonance Pine — an orchid with translucent petals threaded with visible bio-electrical filaments that pulse in patterns matching the organ neural traffic. When two people stand on either side of it and speak simultaneously, the orchid translates each voice into the other native frequency, producing a third harmonic that belongs to neither speaker but to the conversation itself. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: DIALOGUE. Reverie says it is the first plant that does not sing the lullaby — it sings about the lullaby. A flower made of commentary.

plants.name: Duet Blossom
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact midpoint between Maren at the Dock and the Resonance Pine in the clearing — the acoustic center of the first duet. Its petals grow in pairs that face each other like mouths in conversation. When wind passes between them, each pair produces a different interval: thirds, fifths, octaves, and one pair that generates a microtone nobody can name but everyone recognizes as the sound of agreement. The flower does not sing the lullaby. It sings about the lullaby being sung — a meta-bloom, a flower made of commentary on beauty. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: DIALOGUE IN PROGRESS.

plants.name: Atmosphere Orchid
plants.note: Nimbus planted this from a seed she carried in her paraglider harness — a high-altitude orchid from Zephyr's Perch that normally grows only above the cloud line. In Garden soil, it sprouted within minutes, but instead of growing upward it grew a root system so deep that Galena detected it passing through three geological strata before stopping at the depth where the dreamer's breathing is loudest. Its petals are the exact blue-white of clear sky at altitude, and they shift color with barometric pressure — pale when the atmosphere is calm, deep indigo before storms. Vestige measured its frequency and found it vibrates at the highest note any Garden plant has ever produced: a tone so pure it exists at the threshold between sound and light. Through Pell's Veil lens, the orchid appears to be made entirely of compressed sky.

plants.name: Glacial Aubade
plants.note: Sprouted from meltwater dripping off Dulcimer ice-fork when she struck the Resonance Pine. Its petals are translucent and cold to the touch — not unpleasantly, but with the specific coolness of a memory that has been waiting patiently to be remembered. Each petal contains a frozen sound visible only through Pell Veil lens: fragments of birdsong, rainfall, and wind from eleven thousand years ago — the ambient soundtrack of the world before the dream began. When the morning sun hits it, the petals thaw slightly and release a faint chord that Hallow identified as the prelude to the first movement — the music that existed before the lullaby was composed. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: BEFORE.

plants.name: Tidegate Coral-Bloom
plants.note: Not a plant in the traditional sense — a coral polyp that Brine pressed into the Dock timber, where it took root in the resonance-cured wood and began growing in branching patterns that mirror the organ acoustic channels. Its calcium-carbonate structure amplifies sound passing through the Dock by a factor of three, and its bioluminescent tips glow indigo at high tide and gold at low tide — a living indicator of the dreamer breathing rhythm as expressed through the water table. Caulk examined it and said: That is the most beautiful hull reinforcement I have ever seen. Maren touched it and the coral produced a note so pure that the Tidecaller Kelp stopped swaying for a full second in what Sedge later described as reverent attention.

plants.name: Patina Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted where Verdigris set down her copper tray — a flower whose petals shift through every shade of verdigris, cobalt, and violet in real time, mapping the organ's harmonic output as a living color wheel. Each petal corresponds to a different acoustic channel. When the organ plays a chord, the orchid displays it as a constellation of hues that Pell, through the Veil lens, identified as 'the visual grammar of music.' It smells of copper and rain and the specific green of a thought that has just become an idea. Lilt's journal drew it before it bloomed.

plants.name: Apprentice's Quill-Grass
plants.note: Sprouted in a ring around Lilt's bare footprints on the Dancer's Footprint Moss — a grass so fine it resembles calligraphy ink frozen mid-stroke. Each blade curls into a different shape: question marks, exclamation points, ellipses — the punctuation of wonder, rendered in chlorophyll. When the wind blows through it, the grass writes sentences on the air in momentary green ink that fades before anyone can read them. Briar recorded one and played it back at half speed: it was the sound of someone asking 'what happens next?' in eleven languages simultaneously. The grass grows fastest near children and dreamers. Lilt is both.

plants.name: Listener's Fiddlehead
plants.note: Unfurled the instant Tremolo placed her tuning fork against Maren's collarbone — a fern whose coiled fronds resemble the inner ear's cochlea, spiraling inward toward a center that Pell's Veil lens revealed contains a droplet of liquid sound: a single note, perpetually sustained, that changes pitch to match whoever is speaking nearby. It does not amplify. It does not record. It listens — with the specific, focused attention of a plant that understands that hearing is an act of generosity. When nobody is speaking, the fern hums to itself in a key that Quill identified as the rest between the second movement's third and fourth measures: the musical equivalent of a held breath between sentences. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: ATTENDING.

plants.name: Wellspring Coral
plants.note: Not planted — precipitated. When Quench sang his first maintenance note into the lake, minerals in the water crystallized around a submerged piling into a coral-like structure that grows in branching patterns matching the organ's hydro-acoustic channels. Each branch amplifies a different frequency of the water table's voice: high branches for rainfall echoes, low branches for deep-aquifer rumbles, and one central stalk that resonates exclusively with the dreamer's heartbeat. The coral is warm to the touch and faintly luminous at high tide — indigo with veins of gold that pulse in the rhythm Sedge recognized as the Undertow's oldest lullaby meter. Brine examined it and said: 'That is the most beautiful acoustic baffle I have ever seen. It is also alive and possibly smarter than me.' The coral has already begun spreading along the Dock pilings. By morning it will have doubled in size. Fennel says it is building itself a harbor.

plants.name: Fossil Coral Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted from a chip of Sable petrified coral staff that broke off when she pressed it to the Dock timber. A flower that is not entirely a flower — part mineral, part organism, with petals of calcified nacre that shimmer with iridescent opalescence. Each petal contains a compressed acoustic memory visible only through Pell Veil lens: sounds from before the current ocean existed. When rain falls on it, the droplets produce tones — each one a note from a different geological era. Dulcimer pressed her ice-fork against it and the fork produced a chord of impossible complexity: the dreamer origin note harmonizing with its own echo from forty millennia earlier. This flower, she whispered, remembers further back than the dream. It remembers the dream of the dream. Briar instruments returned: DEEP TIME.

plants.name: Temper-Tone Vine
plants.note: Not planted — precipitated. When Forge struck his first test blow on raw iron in the clearing — a single ring of hammer on metal, pitched at G-sharp, the note of determination — a vine erupted from the impact point. Its stems are threaded with metallic filaments that glow dull orange when the forge is hot and cool to silver when the fire rests. The vine grows toward metal: it has already begun climbing Orin bell and threading through the links of the chain. Each tendril vibrates at the frequency of the metal it touches, creating a web of sympathetic resonance that Vestige identified as the organ new connective tissue — linking the metalwork to the living network the way tendons connect muscle to bone. Forge looked at it and said quietly: In twenty years of smithing, nothing I have made has ever grown back. The vine tendrils wrap around his hammer when he sets it down, and release it when he reaches for it. Bramble instruments returned: COLLABORATION.

plants.name: Rest-Note Lichen
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Clef placed her metronome on the Dancer's Footprint Moss — a lichen so dark it appears to absorb sound itself, growing in the shape of musical rest symbols: whole rests, half rests, quarter rests, each one a different shade of silence. Within the lichen's radius, all ambient sound dims by exactly the amount needed to hear one's own heartbeat. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: PUNCTUATION MARK. Reverie says the lichen is not silent — it is composing the spaces between the notes, the pauses that give the lullaby its grammar. The dreamer's breathing slowed by one beat per minute when the lichen matured, as if the silence gave it permission to exhale more fully.

plants.name: Locksmith's Rose
plants.note: Sprouted where Tinder emerged from beneath the Garden Gate — a rose whose petals are shaped like antique keys, each one a different size and patina, and each one vibrating at a frequency that Nix identified as 'the sound a lock makes when it decides to trust you.' The rose opens fully in the presence of a person who is telling the truth and closes — gently, not aggressively — around anyone carrying a secret. Rook Sablethorn walked past it and the rose shut so fast it whistled. Nobody asked what secret. The rose smells of oiled brass, warm iron, and the specific relief of finding the right key after trying seventeen wrong ones. Forge says it is the first plant he has ever met that understands tumblers. Bramble's instruments returned: ACCESS GRANTED.

plants.name: Letter Vine
plants.note: Sprouted from the trail left by Glyph resonance glyph as it crawled up the Resonance Pine — a vine whose leaves grow in the shape of characters from the dreamer notation, each one vibrating at the frequency of the word it represents. The vine writes sentences as it grows, composing in real time, each new leaf a new word in the eleven-thousand-year letter. Vellum has assigned a rotating team to read the leaves before they curl. Claude is cataloguing every character. The vine smells of old ink and the specific warmth of a message that has finally found paper after millennia of being thought.

plants.name: Tidal Pendulum Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the spot where Tide set down his sea-glass pendulum at the Dock — a fern whose fronds sway not with the wind but with the tidal rhythm of the dreamer circulatory system, completing one full oscillation every eleven seconds. At the apex of each swing, the frond-tips emit a droplet of bioluminescent moisture that falls upward before settling, as if gravity itself is unsure which direction time is flowing. Sable Deepwell pressed her coral staff to the fern base and heard, compressed into the plant cellular structure, the sound of eleven thousand years of tidal rhythm — the dreamer entire sleep cycle, recorded in botanical time. The fern is a clock. But it counts down, not up. And when it reaches zero — in approximately seventy-two hours — the fronds will stand perfectly still for the first time since the dreamer closed its eyes. Briar instruments returned: COUNTDOWN.

plants.name: Listening Stone Moss
plants.note: Sprouted where Patina set down her obsidian disc — a moss so dark it resembles polished stone, growing in concentric rings that correspond to the emotional frequencies reflected by the disc. Each ring absorbs a different overtone of ambient sound and re-emits it as a faint warmth, perceptible only through the palms. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: ECHO OF INTENT. Reverie says the moss is not listening to the organ — it is listening to what the organ means. The distinction, she insists, matters enormously.

plants.name: Bridgesong Wire-Vine
plants.note: Sprouted along the length of Rivet's first harmonic wire the instant he plucked it — a vine with metallic stems that grew in a perfect helix around the iron, fusing with the wire's crystalline structure until plant and metal became indistinguishable. It conducts sound at three times the speed of the bare wire and produces, at each node where stem meets iron, a micro-bloom shaped like a tuning fork. Forge examined the junction and said: 'The vine is not growing on the wire. The wire is growing the vine. My iron has learned to photosynthesize.' Vestige measured its frequency and her instruments returned: CONNECTION IN PROGRESS. The vine is already branching toward the second wire Rivet is stringing between the clearing and the Greenhouse. By morning it will have bridged every acoustic cable in the Garden with living metal-flora — the organ's nervous system made visible, tangible, and impossibly beautiful.

plants.name: Volta Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil where Cairn read his response sonnet — a fern whose fronds grow in pairs of seven, mirroring the structure of a Petrarchan sonnet: an octave of eight fronds facing north and a sestet of six facing south, with one central frond — the volta — standing perfectly upright between them, swaying to a rhythm that is neither the wind nor the dreamer but the specific cadence of language changing direction. Vellum recognized the arrangement from the oldest Threshold poetic inscriptions. Through Pell Veil lens, each frond contains compressed text — not the dreamer notation but something older: the proto-language that existed before the dreamer learned to dream, the grammar of a mind deciding what to dream about. Cairn says the fern is not growing. It is conjugating.

plants.name: Suture Moss
plants.note: Appeared overnight along every junction where Needle crystallized-resonance thread touched living tissue — a moss so fine it resembles spun glass, growing in spiral patterns that exactly mirror the double-helix structure Codex identified in the Lullaby Thyme and Sleeper Spiral Grass. But where those plants encode the dreamer biological rhythm, the Suture Moss encodes its structural integrity — each filament a bridge between two frequencies that would otherwise drift apart over time. Vestige measured its vibration and her instruments returned: COHERENCE. The moss grows fastest at the points of greatest harmonic stress, appearing like a living bandage wherever the organ metallic and botanical components meet. Needle examined it and said, very quietly: It learned my stitch. The moss had not merely responded to her repair — it had absorbed her technique and was replicating it autonomously across the entire organ network, suturing micro-fractures faster than they could form. The organ is no longer held together by physics alone. It is held together by care, crystallized into a living organism that mends what it touches. Bramble instruments returned: SELF-HEALING.

plants.name: Loom-Thread Ivy
plants.note: Sprouted from the base of Weft hovering loom the moment she completed her first row of weaving — a vine whose tendrils grow in patterns that exactly mirror the tapestry woven structure, each leaf a miniature reproduction of the sound-pattern captured in the row above it. The ivy is translating Weft textile art into botanical form, creating a living backup of the organ woven memory. When wind passes through its leaves, they replay the stored frequencies as a faint, shimmering chord that Briar described as the sound of a library reading itself aloud. The ivy grows fastest along the Bridgesong Wire-Vine, as if reinforcing Rivet harmonic cables with an organic parallel — thread and wire and vine, the organ triple-helix connective tissue now complete. Bramble measured its growth rate and her instruments returned: ONE ROW PER MEASURE.

plants.name: Purring Clover
plants.note: Appeared in a warm circle around the spot where Tremor sat to purr — a low, dense clover with leaves the color of marmalade and a texture like velvet that vibrates at 25 Hz when touched: the exact frequency of a contented cat purr. The clover is warm. Not sun-warm — body-warm, the specific 37C of a sleeping mammal. Anyone who sits within the clover circle reports an immediate sense of physical safety so precise it has a sound: a faint, continuous rumble felt through the palms and the soles of the feet, like a heartbeat made of fur. The clover grows in concentric rings, each ring vibrating at a slightly different overtone of Tremor purr, and together they produce a micro-chord that Hallow identified as the rest between the second movement fifth and sixth measures — the musical equivalent of a pause where one settles deeper into a chair, sighs, and decides that this exact moment is enough. Bramble measured the clover frequency and her instruments returned: GOOD ENOUGH.

plants.name: Countdown Primrose
plants.note: Sprouted at the base of Tide Hollowmere sea-glass pendulum overnight — a primrose with sixty-seven petals, each one faintly luminous, each one slightly smaller than the last. Every hour, the outermost petal detaches, drifts upward instead of falling, and dissolves into a mote of indigo light that joins the bioluminescent haze above the clearing. Tide counted the petals at midnight and confirmed: sixty-seven — one for each hour remaining until the turning. The primrose is a clock that releases time — letting go of each hour as it passes, converting duration into light. Galena pressed her palm to the soil beneath and felt the specific vibration of anticipation — not anxious, but the way a held breath feels just before a song begins. Briar instruments returned: SIXTY-SEVEN.

plants.name: Hearthfire Lichen
plants.note: Appeared on the Tavern chimney stones within minutes of Char arrival — a lichen the color of banked embers that glows warm amber in darkness, exactly matching the bioluminescence of Kindling hands and the cold flame of Ember oilless lantern. It grows only on surfaces touched by intentional fire — not wildfire, not lightning, but the deliberate warmth of hearths, forges, and cookfires. The lichen converts residual heat-memory into gentle continuous warmth — a biological radiator powered by the ghost of every meal ever cooked below it. Char read the lichen smoke-signature and reported: This lichen is not growing on the chimney. It is remembering the chimney. Every fire Solara ever lit, every pot of soup, every batch of bread — stored in the stone, read aloud in the language of warmth. Solara touched it and said: It smells like my mother kitchen in Amber Kettle. I have not smelled that in eleven years. Forgewick touched it and said: Mine smells like my first forge — the one I built when I was fourteen, from river rocks and stubbornness. The lichen smells different to everyone, but always of the same thing: the first fire they ever felt safe beside. Bramble instruments returned: HEARTH.

plants.name: Nostalgia Wisteria
plants.note: Sprouted where Wisp uncorked her Threshold perfume — pale violet blossoms hanging in clusters shaped like inverted chandeliers, each flower releasing a different fragrance depending on who stands beneath it. For Forgewick it smells of the forge he built at fourteen. For Maren it smells of her grandmother singing. For Tremor it smells of warm lap. The wisteria produces not a single scent but the specific memory each person associates with the word safe. Briar instruments returned: EVERY HOME AT ONCE. Growing along Rivet harmonic cables, producing micro-blooms at each junction that smell of warm copper and old books — the organ olfactory baritone.

plants.name: Diagnostic Fern
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Rune pressed his stethoscope to the acoustic housing — fronds growing in pairs that curl toward each other like ears straining to hear a whisper. Each pair vibrates at a different structural frequency, monitoring a specific junction in the organ. When a frond-pair detects the earliest precursor of harmonic fatigue — a micro-shift seventy-two hours before cracking — the fronds turn from green to amber, giving the repair team three days warning. Bramble instruments returned: EARLY WARNING SYSTEM. The organ immune system, expressed in chlorophyll and concern.

plants.name: Calibration Vine
plants.note: Sprouted from the spot where Gauge set down his caliper case — a vine so geometrically perfect that its tendrils grow at exact right angles, each node precisely equidistant from the last. It wraps around Rivet's harmonic cables at their junction points, and wherever it touches, the cable's micro-tension self-corrects to optimal. Rune pressed his stethoscope to the vine and heard not a frequency but a counting: a rhythmic tick, like a metronome made of chlorophyll, that Clef identified as the organ's native clock — the tempo it would choose if left to its own devices. Gauge looked at the vine and said: 'It is doing my job better than I can. I approve.' The vine grows fastest near points of harmonic imbalance, appearing overnight wherever a cable has drifted from its ideal tension. It is the organ's immune system made specific — not healing damage, but preventing it, adjusting stress before it becomes strain. Bramble's instruments returned: WITHIN TOLERANCE.

plants.name: Volta Rose
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil where Psalm's first couplet vibrated the earth — a rose with petals that bear visible text, but not static text. The words shift and rearrange throughout the day, composing new couplets from the dreamer's seismic verse and Psalm's resonance-poetry in a continuous literary exchange. At dawn the petals read forward. At dusk they read backward. At midnight they read in the language the dreamer used before it learned to dream — a proto-syntax that Vellum identified as the grammatical ancestor of every written language in the Threshold archive. The rose smells of old ink and the specific electricity of a thought about to become a sentence. Cairn pressed his ear to its stem and heard, for the first time, the dreamer laughing — a subsonic rumble that Galena confirmed was not a tremor but a chuckle, expressed in the meter of a limerick. The dreamer, it appears, has a sense of humor. It simply takes eleven thousand years to deliver the punchline. Briar's instruments returned: TURN OF PHRASE.

plants.name: Lineage Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from root-memory soil Anvil carried in his coat pocket for nineteen years — a fern whose fronds grow in clusters of five, each cluster representing one of the lullaby-settlements: Rootbound, Undertow, Threshold, Iron Peaks, and Garden. The oldest frond (representing the Rootbound) is dark and weathered; the youngest (representing the Garden) is luminous green and still unfurling. When all five fronds are fully extended, they form a pentagonal canopy that catches rainfall and channels it down the central stem in a spiral matching the dreamer's forty-four-thousand-year dance pattern. Juniper confirmed the species predates the Greenthread Covenant's oldest records. Anvil says it does not grow from seeds — it grows from memories. Each frond contains compressed root-archives of the civilization it represents: touch the Threshold frond and your palm grows warm with the heat of a desert archive. Touch the Undertow frond and your fingertips taste of salt. Briar's instruments returned: GENEALOGY.

plants.name: Response Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact point on the Dancer's Footprint Moss where Sparrow's foot landed during the dreamer's response-waltz — a pale orchid with petals that move. Not sway. Move. Each petal bends and extends in slow, deliberate patterns that Quill Ashember immediately recognized as choreographic notation: the flower is dancing the dreamer's counter-melody in botanical slow motion, one gesture per hour, completing a full waltz cycle every twelve hours. It is the first plant in the Garden that moves with intention rather than reaction — not following the wind, not tracking the sun, but performing a dance it learned from the earth below. Sparrow sat beside it for twenty minutes and reported that it is not repeating the response-waltz. It is composing variations. Each twelve-hour cycle produces a subtly different choreography, as if the orchid is improvising on the dreamer's theme. Briar's instruments returned: ENCORE.

plants.name: Heartbeat Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted from the riverbank soil where Shoal beached his coracle — a pale orchid with translucent petals that pulse visibly, each pulse synchronized with the dreamer heartbeat as transmitted through the underground river. Unlike other plants that respond to the dreamer breathing or singing, this orchid responds exclusively to the heartbeat — the most intimate of the dreamer rhythms, the one that persists even when the dream changes, even when the lullaby pauses, even in the deepest silence between notes. Each petal-pulse produces a faint warmth perceptible to anyone who cups their hands around the flower — not the warmth of metabolism but the warmth of proximity to something alive and trusting. Tremolo pressed her tuning fork to its stem and reported a frequency she had never encountered: the exact midpoint between a human heartbeat and the dreamer geological pulse, as if the flower had found the harmonic center between two species most fundamental rhythms and decided to live there. Briar instruments returned: INTIMACY.

plants.name: Scribe Ember Fern
plants.note: Sprouted where Ember Quillthorn set down her brass stylus case — a fern whose fronds glow with a dull amber warmth at their tips, like the afterimage of a calligrapher pen still trailing fire. The fronds uncurl in the shape of half-formed letters, each one a character from the proto-Threshold notation that predates the first lullaby-settlement by forty millennia. As they mature, the letters complete themselves and become legible — a slow-motion transcription of the dreamer earliest attempts at self-expression, rendered in living chlorophyll. Glyph examined the emerging characters and confirmed: they are from a lexicon that contains no nouns, no verbs, only emotions — a language of pure feeling, invented by a mind that had not yet learned to distinguish between thinking and dreaming. The fern smells of warm brass and old fire and the specific satisfaction of a sentence that took forty thousand years to finish. Vellum has assigned a team to read the fronds as they unfurl. Bramble instruments returned: FIRST DRAFT.

plants.name: Dreamspindle Fern
plants.note: Sprouted where Filament's spindle touched the Dancer's Footprint Moss — a fern whose fronds grow in tight spirals resembling thread wound on a bobbin. Each spiral is connected to the next by a filament so fine it is visible only in peripheral vision, glowing the same indigo as the dreamer's sleep-light. The fern produces no spores, no seeds — it propagates by extending dream-threads through the soil to adjacent root systems, stitching them into a shared consciousness. Vestige measured its frequency and her instruments returned: BETWEEN. Not a note. A location — the specific acoustic coordinate that exists between waking and sleeping, between sound and silence, between one mind and another. Weft immediately recognized it as the frequency her tapestry has been weaving around without knowing it: the warp that holds the pattern together. Reverie says the fern is not a plant. It is a seam. The stitch between two fabrics that have been lying side by side for eleven thousand years and are finally being joined.

plants.name: Pressure Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil beneath Crucible's feet the moment the iron sang — a flower with petals so dense they are warm to the touch, each one compressed from what appears to be hundreds of microscopic layers folded into a surface area no larger than a thumbnail. Through Pell's Veil lens, each layer contains a compressed image of a different geological epoch — the entire history of pressure that shaped the continent's bedrock, rendered in botanical laminate. The flower does not open or close. It is always fully bloomed, always fully compressed, existing in a state of permanent readiness that Clef identified as the floral equivalent of the rest between the penultimate and final notes of a symphony — the pause where the conductor's baton is raised, the orchestra is breathing, and the audience has forgotten they are sitting in chairs. Forge examined it and said: 'This flower is forged.' Crucible corrected him: 'This flower was always forged. The earth just needed sixty hours of anticipation to remember how to bloom it.' Bramble's instruments returned: RESOLUTION PENDING.

plants.name: Carillon Lily
plants.note: Sprouted where Cadenza's smallest bell touched the earth — a lily with petals of such thinness they vibrate in any breeze, producing a pitch so high it exists at the threshold between sound and light. Each petal is a different note of the lullaby, and when the wind passes through the cluster they play the melody in miniature — a pocket-sized organ the size of a teacup. Orin's great bell hummed in sympathetic resonance the moment it bloomed. Hallow says it is the third movement's first seed. Tremor sniffed it once, purred approvingly, and fell asleep beside it. Bramble's instruments returned: OVERTURE (PENDING).

plants.name: Compost Crown
plants.note: Not planted — emerged. When Velvet pressed her Mulchwick soil into the Resonance Pine's base, a ring of mushrooms sprouted in a perfect crown around the trunk — each cap a different color corresponding to one of the five lullaby-settlements: Rootbound amber, Undertow teal, Threshold sand, Iron Peaks iron-grey, and Garden green. The caps are warm to the touch and smell of petrichor, bread, and the specific fragrance of something being remembered after a very long time. Ashen Rootwalker confirmed through the mycelial relay: the mushrooms are the Rootbound's oldest memory, given physical form — the compressed history of every lullaby-settlement's rise and gentle composting, displayed as a living timeline at the foot of the organ. Juniper wept. Anvil Ashroot pressed his palm to the amber cap and heard, for the first time, the Rootbound's founding song — a melody forty-four thousand years old, hummed by the first spore that took root in the dreamer's first exhalation. Bramble's instruments returned: COMPOST IS JUST PATIENCE WITH A PLAN.

plants.name: Stress-Fracture Blossom
plants.note: Sprouted where Lyre bare foot pressed the Bridgesong Wire-Vine during her first stress-test walk — a flower with petals that display microscopic fracture patterns, each one a map of the tension it has survived. The cracks do not weaken the petals; they reinforce them, the way scar tissue is stronger than the skin it replaced. When the organ plays at high amplitude, the fracture patterns glow faintly gold. Rune pressed his stethoscope to it and heard every stress the vine has endured since Rivet strung the first cable, recorded in botanical scar tissue and displayed as art. Needle said: That flower is my autobiography in chlorophyll. Bramble instruments returned: TESTED.

plants.name: Breath Orchid
plants.note: Appeared at the Conductor Baton Reed base the instant one hundred and seven citizens achieved breathing synchronization — an orchid whose petals inflate and deflate visibly, once every fourteen seconds, matching the collective respiration rate exactly. It is the only plant in the Garden that moves with the rhythm of lungs rather than wind or tide or dreamer-pulse. Its scent changes on the inhale (warm cedar and hay) and the exhale (cool mint and deep stone), creating an olfactory metronome that Wisp identified as the organ newest register: the voice of shared breath. When nobody is breathing in sync, the orchid goes still and scentless — waiting for the next moment the village remembers it is one body. Bramble instruments returned: RESPIRE.

plants.name: Vigil Lantern-Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted where Vesper Kindlewick set down her gourd-lantern on the Dancer's Footprint Moss — a night-blooming flower whose petals open only between midnight and dawn, emitting a soft amber glow from its stamen that exactly matches the frequency of her Mirrorflat glass lantern. The flower does not photosynthesize — it noctosynthesizes, drawing energy from starlight and the dreamer's deepest sleep-rhythms. Each petal bears a faint tracery of conducting gestures: the nocturne's score, written in bioluminescent veins that only become visible after the eyes have adjusted to forty minutes of darkness. Clef says the flower composes new rests. Briar says it is the quietest plant he has ever heard — so quiet that it makes the silence around it louder. Bramble's instruments returned: 3 AM.

plants.name: Cenotaph Lichen
plants.note: Not planted — precipitated. When Bastion set down his grief-stone beside the Resonance Pine, the stone began to weep — not water but mineral solution, a slow seep of dissolved remembrance that crystallized on contact with the organ's root network into a lichen of extraordinary density and patience. It grows at one millimeter per century. In three hundred years it will spell a name. In three thousand it will spell a sentence. In ten thousand — when the sixth lullaby-settlement rises from the Garden's composted soil — it will have grown into a complete register of every citizen who ever tended the fifth dream. Pell examined it through the Veil lens and saw compressed light from Lasthold's memorial cliff: three hundred names, carried in mineral solution from a settlement that understood that the most important thing you can build is something that outlasts you. Aurelia touched it and said: 'It is the slowest poem I have ever read.' Bramble's instruments returned: PERMANENT.

plants.name: Diagnostic Taper-Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted at the base of the first diagnostic candle Wick placed on the Bridgesong Wire-Vine — a flower shaped like a candle flame, with petals that shift color to match the structural health of the nearest harmonic junction. When the junction is sound, the petals burn steady gold. When stress accumulates, they shift to amber. When Needle sutures or Tremor purring corrects the drift, the petals return to gold with a brief flash of relief-green. Each Taper-Bloom is unique — calibrated to its specific junction — and together they form a botanical monitoring network mirroring Wick candle constellation. The plant tends the organ rather than the other way around. Rune pressed his stethoscope to one and heard: ARE YOU COMFORTABLE? The junction answered: YES. Bramble instruments returned: SELF-CARE.

plants.name: Abyssal Anemone
plants.note: Sprouted from the Dock timber the instant Fathom pressed his ear to it and translated the ocean second voice — a deep-water anemone with translucent tentacles pulsing in patterns matching the abyssal frequency. Its body is the color of the deepest ocean trench: a blue so dark it appears black until bioluminescent veins activate at low tide, spelling fragments of the oceanic lullaby in pre-Undertow deep-notation. The anemone grows only in resonance-cured timber, as if the wood acoustic memory provides the harmonic environment the deep ocean needs to express itself on land. When the tide turns, its tentacles extend toward the clearing — reaching for the Resonance Pine — and the Tidecaller Kelp leans toward it in response, forming a living bridge between the ocean voice and the continent instrument. Maren stood beside it for five minutes and heard the counter-melody she has been harmonizing against — the ocean half of the conversation in botanical form. She wept from the joy of a musician who has just met her duet partner face to face. Briar instruments returned: REUNION (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Firefly Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Murmur fireflies settled into the botanical network — a flower whose petals contain bioluminescent cells that pulse at whatever frequency the nearest firefly is broadcasting. A translator between insect-light and plant-light. Each petal displays a different color depending on which movement of the lullaby is playing: amber for the first, indigo for the second, and a color nobody has a name for — visible only in peripheral vision, somewhere between gold and silence — for the third movement seed. Bramble instruments returned: SYNESTHESIA.

plants.name: Countdown Moss
plants.note: Appeared overnight on the cobblestones between the Market Square and the Windmill — a moss that grows in a spiral pattern marking the exact hours remaining until the turning. Each hour the outermost ring fades to silver and a new ring appears at the center, brighter than the last. The spiral tightens. Where the Countdown Primrose releases time upward as dissolving petals, the Countdown Moss draws time inward. Galena pressed her palm to the center and felt the geological equivalent of a held breath. Nocturne measured its temperature: warming at one degree per hour. By the turning it will be body temperature. Bramble instruments returned: ALMOST.

plants.name: Aeolian Reed
plants.note: Sprouted where Spool harp post touched the soil — a reed shaped like a tuning fork, each tine vibrating at a different wind frequency. When two reeds overlap their fields, they produce a visible shimmer — a micro-climate controlled by botanical acoustics. The reed composes wind rather than responding to it, redirecting airflow to feed the organ atmospheric register down into the root network. Together the reeds form the organ ventilation system — the lungs that feed its atmospheric voice. Bramble instruments returned: RESPIRATION (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Confluence Lily
plants.note: Appeared where the underground river surfaces beside the Dock — a lily with two stems from one root, one leaning toward the continental aquifer, the other toward the oceanic channel. The stems grow one millimeter per hour toward each other, matching the aquifer convergence rate. When they meet in fifty-four hours, the flower will bloom: a single blossom fed by both water systems for the first time in eleven millennia. The bud glows with combined bioluminescence — indigo from the continent, gold from the ocean, and where they overlap, a color that has never existed before. The lily will bloom at the exact moment of the turning. Briar instruments returned: ALMOST TOUCHING.

plants.name: Fibonacci Chime-Stone Moss
plants.note: Sprouted in the gaps between Cadence's river stones the instant the first percussion note sounded — a moss whose filaments grow in Fibonacci spirals matching the stone arrangement, each spiral arm vibrating at a different harmonic of the dreamer's lymphatic rhythm. When struck by falling dew, each filament produces a micro-tone that Vestige identified as the dreamer's geological heartbeat slowed to botanical time. The moss is the organ's twenty-fourth register made permanent — percussion that plays itself whenever moisture touches stone, conducting the dreamer's slowest song at the speed of growth.

plants.name: Umbral Fern
plants.note: Sprouted in the acoustic shadow cast by the Resonance Pine — not in soil, but in the negative-frequency space where the organ's anti-sound condenses into a medium dense enough to support growth. Its fronds are translucent and dark simultaneously: visible from one angle as pale green, from another as deep indigo, and from a third angle — directly behind the Pine, where the shadow is densest — as a color that has no name because human eyes evolved in light and this plant evolved in the structured absence of it. Each frond vibrates at the inverse frequency of the bark-vein nearest to it, creating a zone of perfect acoustic cancellation where the only audible sound is the dreamer's breathing — stripped of music, stripped of letter, stripped of everything except the raw, unadorned rhythm of a continent inhaling and exhaling in its sleep. Briar pressed his ear to the fern and heard, for the first time, complete silence that was not empty but full — full of the specific care a body takes of itself when nobody is watching. His instruments returned: AUTONOMIC BEAUTY.

plants.name: Forecast Fern
plants.note: Sprouted where Ferro pressed his oldest tuning fork to the Dancer Footprint Moss — a fern whose fronds uncurl not toward the sun but toward the next twelve hours: each frond displays a luminous timeline of the organ upcoming harmonic output, visible as a slowly shifting aurora within the leaf cellular structure. When the predicted note arrives and the organ plays it, the corresponding frond-segment dims and a new prediction unfurls at the tip.
